content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street ♦ Suite 620 ♦ Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 ♦ Fax (916) 322-0886

June 1, 2010

Michaelyn Jones
General Counsel
Santa Monica Rent Control Board
1685 Main Street
P.O. Box 2200
Santa Monica, CA 90407-2200

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-10-051a

Dear Ms. Jones:

This letter responds to your request for reconsideration of advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act")¹ provided in our previous advice letter to you, on behalf of Santa Monica Rent Control Board Commissioner Robert Kronovet, dated April 29, 2010. (Jones Advice Letter, No. A-10-051.)

Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that may have already taken place, and conclusions in this letter apply only to prospective actions. In addition, this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission ("the Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

For the purposes of this letter, the facts presented in your previous request for advice dated April 29, 2009, have been incorporated herein by reference. (Jones Advice Letter, supra.)

You have added the following relevant facts to your request: There are 22,061 property owners in Santa Monica and 20,734 residential property owners. There are 3,036 owners of rent controlled properties.

You also state that, "It is highly unlikely that a 4.2% increase will be granted this year or at any time in the near future." You also stated that since the issuance of our prior advice to you that "staff has recommended a two percent increase for consideration by the Board." You also

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-10-051a
Page No. 2

said it was "unlikely, if not impossible, that the Board's decision would exceed two percent." If
the board approves a one percent increase, the amount of income to Commissioner Kronovet
from his six residential rental units would be $883.68.² If a two percent increase is approved,
the increase to the property's income producing potential would be $1,767.36³ annually.

In addition, you state that on May 19, 2010, Commissioner Kronovet signed a waiver
agreeing not to take the 2010/2011 annual general adjustment, and that "by doing so, he believes
he has removed any possible conflict of interest having to do with his ownership of the six-unit
residential property . . . ."

Based on the new facts provided, you ask reconsideration with regard to whether:

(1) The presumption of non-materiality for Commissioner Kronovet's real
property interest is rebutted pursuant to Regulation 18705.2(b) due to the
possible effects on the income-producing potential of the real property;

(2) Whether the public generally exception applies to Commissioner Kronovet's
circumstances;

(3) Whether Commissioner Kronovet's waiver of the 2010/2011 General
Adjustment has any effect on his legal obligation to disqualify himself from the
annual rent increase decision.

Income Producing Potential of Real Property and the Waiver:

You state that Regulation 18705.2(b) "gives no indication of a threshold amount that
would rise to the standard of rebutting the presumption" of materiality with regard to an
indirectly involved property's "income producing potential." Therefore, you ask that the
application of the income producing criteria in this regulation "should not be applied to
Commissioner Kronovet's economic interest . . . ." You also state that you do not think that the
presumption is rebutted and that "the decision regarding the annual rent increase will not have a
reasonably foreseeable material effect on Commissioner Kronovet's residential rental property."

Commissioner Kronovet's waiver, if valid and legally enforceable,⁴ would have a bearing
on the income producing potential of his six residential rental property units as otherwise
permitted if the annual general adjustment is passed by the board. Therefore, based on the
waiver, the impact on Commissioner Kronovet's property may not be sufficient to rebut the

² The current combined monthly maximum allowable rents equal $7,364 plus 1 percent equals $73.64.
The amount $73.64 x 12 months equals $883.68.

³ The current combined monthly maximum allowable rents equal $7,364 plus 2 percent equals $147.28.
The amount $147.28 x 12 months equals $1,767.36.

⁴ Because of our ultimate conclusion that the Annual General Adjustment will still have an impact on the
value of the real property, we do not offer an opinion as to the validity or enforceability of the waiver as it relates to
the adjustment and the rental property.

File No. A-10-051a
Page No. 3

presumption of nonmateriality with regard to indirectly involved property. You also contend,
however, that because it is unlikely the board will approve a rental adjustment of more than two
percent, the decision on the adjustment will not reach the required materiality threshold for
income even if his waiver is not valid or enforceable. However, without more facts concerning
exactly how the adjustment will be presented to the board for decision, we cannot accept your
contention. For example, if the motion is presented to the board as a two percent adjustment but
Commissioner Kronovet and other board members think the percentage should be higher, a "no"
vote by Mr. Kronovet could result in a rejection of the proposed two percent adjustment and the
imposition of a higher percentage even though he does not participate in the vote on the higher
rate. Therefore, although a valid and enforceable waiver by Commissioner Kronovet may
diminish income as a factor requiring his disqualification, based on your letter, we do not agree
that a decision solely on a two percent adjustment removes income as a factor.

Nevertheless, as we stated in our response to Commissioner Kronovet's separate request
for reconsideration (Kronovet Advice Letter, A-10-093), it is but one factor considered in
determining whether it is reasonably foreseeable that a decision will have a material financial
effect on an official's real property interest.

In our original letter, we said the waiver, on its face, does not completely and
unequivocally neutralize the material financial benefits of the annual rent increase decision and
its effects generally on property values of rent-controlled real estate in the city. In other words,
regardless of whether Commissioner Kronovet receives additional income from the Annual
General Adjustment for 2010-2011, we believe the decision would still have an impact on the
value of his real property. Moreover, because Commissioner Kronovet owns six residential rental
property units, this financial effect would be substantially magnified with regard to his property
interests as compared to the financial effect on property owners with only one property. (Enright
Advice Letter, No. A-10-006.)

Therefore, reviewing the additional facts you present and the applicable law, we reaffirm
the conclusion reached in the Jones Advice Letter, supra that that Commissioner Kronovet has a
disqualifying conflict of interest and may not make, participate in making, or influence any
government decisions allowing residential rental property owners in the city an annual rent
increase because the decision would have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on his
economic interest in six residential rental units.

Public Generally Exception

However, Commissioner Kronovet may nevertheless participate in the governmental
decision in which he has a disqualifying conflict-of-interest if he meets the requirements of the
basic public generally exception under Regulation 18707.1.

Under that Regulation, he must meet a two-part test by showing that the decision would
affect a "significant segment" of the public in "substantially the same manner" as it financially
affects his economic interest. For decisions that affect a public official's real property,
"significant segment" is defined as 10-percent or more of all property owners or all residential

File No. A-10-051a
Page No. 4

property owners in Santa Monica, or 5,000 property owners or residential property owners in the city.

Your facts indicate there are 22,061 property owners in Santa Monica and 20,734 residential property owners. In addition, there are 3,036 owners of rent controlled properties, and there are 492 six-unit, rent-controlled properties in the city.

Based on your facts, it appears that a "significant segment" will be affected by the annual rent increase decision because 10 percent of all property owners in the city equal 2,206, while 10 percent of all residential property owners in the city equal 2,073. You indicate that 3,016 owners of rent controlled properties will be affected by this decision. Therefore you have identified a significant segment of the public with respect to the governmental decision in question and this factor is met.

The next step is to determine whether the "significant segment" will be affected in substantially the same manner as Commissioner Kronovet's economic interest. A number of factors are considered in determining whether a an economic interest in real property will be financially affected in a manner substantially similar to the significant segment including, but not limited to: the location of the property, the impact on the property due to development potential, the size of the property, and the number of units/parcels owned by the official compared to others in the significant segment. (Regulation 18707.1(b)(2)(A).)

Your facts indicate that Commissioner Kronovet owns a six-unit residential property and there are 492 six-unit, rent-controlled properties in the city. In order to satisfy this prong of the analysis, at least ten percent of the city's property owners or 10 percent of all residential property owners in the city must be impacted in substantially the same manner. (Berger Advice Letter, No. A-05-054.)

Based on the facts you present it appears unlikely that Commissioner Kronovet's economic interest in real property will be affected in "substantially the same manner" as the significant segment identified above (i.e. the 2,206 property owners in Santa Monica, or the 2,073 residential property owners in the city).⁵

Therefore, you have not provided us enough information to indicate that the public generally exception would apply to Commissioner Kronovet's circumstances. Accordingly, Commissioner Kronovet may not make, participate in making, or influence decisions regarding the annual rent increase.

_____________

⁵ For instance, in the Shirey Advice Letter, No. A-06-132, we stated that "given the fact that there are two properties owned by the Commissioner, it is unlikely that the dollar value impact on these two properties will be substantially be same as on property owners' homes where they only own one home."

File No. A-10-051a
Page No. 5


If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

By: Emelyn Rodriguez
Counsel, Legal Division

ER:jgl

sections.question: Based on the new facts provided, you ask reconsideration with regard to whether: (1) The presumption of non-materiality for commissioner Kronovet's real property interest is rebutted pursuant to Regulation 18705.2(b) due to the possible effects on the income-producing potential of the real property; (2) Whether the public generally exception applies to Commissioner Kronovet's circumstances; (3) whether commissioner Kronovet's waiver of the 2010/2011 General Adjustment has any effect on his legal obligation to disqualify himself from the annual rent increase decision.
sections.conclusion: Therefore, reviewing the additional facts you present and the applicable law, we reaffirm the conclusion reached in the Jones Advice Letter, supra that that Commissioner Kronovet has a disqualifying conflict of interest and may not make, participate in making, or influence any government decisions allowing residential rental property owners in the city on annual rent increase because the decision would have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on his economic interest in six residential rental units. However, Commissioner Kronovet may nevertheless participate in the governmental decision in which he has a disqualifying conflict-of-interest if he meets the requirements of the basic public generally exception under Regulation 19707.1. However, based on your facts, you have not provided us enough information to indicate that the public generally exception would apply to Commissioner Kronovet's circumstances. Accordingly, Commissioner Kronovet may not make, participate in making, or influence decisions regarding the annual rent increase.

a
sections.question_synthetic: Whether Santa Monica Rent Control Board Commissioner Kronovet must disqualify himself from voting on annual rent increases for his six residential rental units, considering his signed waiver of the 2010/2011 adjustment, the presumption of materiality under regulations, and whether the public generally exception applies to his circumstances.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Commissioner Kronovet has a disqualifying conflict of interest and cannot vote on annual rent increase decisions because the decision would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on his six residential rental properties. The signed waiver does not eliminate the conflict because the rental adjustment decision would still impact property values. The public generally exception does not apply because his economic interest (six-unit property) would not be affected in substantially the same manner as the broader segment of all property owners in the city.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: OCR document with minor formatting issues. This is a reconsideration letter (A-10-051A) responding to an earlier advice letter (A-10-051). Clear question and conclusion sections present. Some OCR artifacts noted (e.g., 'PRACI'TCES' for 'PRACTICES', 'odifi6i11.nt' for 'ordinance') but meaning is recoverable. The letter references another concurrent advice letter (Kronovet A-10-093) and prior correspondence.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Based on the new facts provided, you ask reconsideration with regard to whether: (1) The presumption of non-materiality for commissioner Kronovet's real property interest is rebutted pursuant to Regulation 18705.2(b) due to the possible effects on the income-producing potential of the real property; (2) Whether the public generally exception applies to Commissioner Kronovet's circumstances; (3) whether commissioner Kronovet's waiver of the 2010/2011 General Adjustment has any effect on his legal obligation to disqualify himself from the annual rent increase decision.

CONCLUSION: Therefore, reviewing the additional facts you present and the applicable law, we reaffirm the conclusion reached in the Jones Advice Letter, supra that that Commissioner Kronovet has a disqualifying conflict of interest and may not make, participate in making, or influence any government decisions allowing residential rental property owners in the city on annual rent increase because the decision would have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on his economic interest in six residential rental units. However, Commissioner Kronovet may nevertheless participate in the governmental decision in which he has a disqualifying conflict-of-interest if he meets the requirements of the basic public generally exception under Regulation 19707.1. However, based on your facts, you have not provided us enough information to indicate that the public generally exception would apply to Commissioner Kronovet's circumstances. Accordingly, Commissioner Kronovet may not make, participate in making, or influence decisions regarding the annual rent increase.

embedding.summary: This FPPC advice letter reconsiders a conflict of interest matter regarding Santa Monica Rent Control Board Commissioner Kronovet's participation in annual rent increase decisions affecting his six residential rental units. The Commission reaffirms that Kronovet must disqualify himself due to a material financial conflict and finds that the public generally exception does not apply to his circumstances.
